stories.headline: Shooting at Mall of Louisiana Injures 10 in Baton Rouge
stories.summary:
At least 10 people were injured Thursday afternoon in a shooting at the Mall of Louisiana in Baton Rouge, Louisiana, officials said. Governor Jeff Landry confirmed he was made aware of an active shooter scene at the mall, and videos circulating on social media showed a large law enforcement response with multiple police vehicles. At least two of the injured were reported in critical condition, with the shooting occurring in the mall's food court.

Authorities have not yet publicly identified a suspect or announced any arrests as of Thursday afternoon. The incident prompted a major emergency response from local and state law enforcement. The mall was evacuated as police worked to secure the scene.

The shooting comes one day after a man from North Carolina was arrested at a Florida hotel while allegedly en route to open fire at the New Orleans Jazz & Heritage Festival, which began Thursday. The proximity of these two incidents has heightened public safety concerns across Louisiana. Governor Landry, a Republican, issued a statement from his office as the situation developed.

The motive for the mall shooting remains under investigation. Authorities have not yet released information on the identities of the victims or whether the shooter is still at large. Further details are expected at a law enforcement press conference later Thursday.

stories.headline: Federal Authorities Arrest More Than Two Dozen Mexican Mafia Members Across Southern California
stories.summary:
Federal authorities arrested more than two dozen members and associates of the Mexican Mafia on Thursday during a sweeping early morning crackdown across Southern California, primarily in Orange County. The FBI and partner agencies executed search and arrest warrants at approximately 30 locations, part of an investigation that has resulted in a total of 43 individuals, including those already in custody, being indicted on charges including murder, kidnapping, extortion, illegal gambling and drug trafficking.

Officers seized 120 pounds of methamphetamine, more than eight pounds of fentanyl, 25 firearms and over $30,000 in cash during the operation. Court documents allege that one incarcerated leader used contraband cellphones to direct criminal activities from a California state prison cell between June 2024 and April 2026, ordering street gang members to kidnap and assault people and overseeing the sale of fentanyl, meth, heroin and cocaine.

The indictment also alleges the gang ran illegal gambling businesses inside commercial strip malls and private residences throughout Orange County, collecting extortionate payments and providing violent enforcement. The Mexican Mafia is suspected in connection with a murder at a gang-controlled motel in Anaheim, according to prosecutors. First Assistant U.S. Attorney Bill Essayli said at a news conference that the seized drugs and weapons posed severe dangers to the public.

The Mexican Mafia was founded in the 1950s inside a California juvenile detention facility and grew into an international criminal organization that exerts control over drug sales, smuggling and extortion operations from within the state prison system. Defendants began making initial appearances in federal court in Los Angeles and Orange counties Thursday afternoon.

stories.headline: DOJ Reclassifies State-Licensed Medical Marijuana to Schedule III, Easing Federal Restrictions
stories.summary:
Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche on Thursday ordered the immediate reclassification of FDA-approved and state-licensed marijuana from Schedule I to Schedule III under the Controlled Substances Act. The move places medical marijuana in the same regulatory category as ketamine, anabolic steroids, and Tylenol with codeine — drugs considered to have moderate to low potential for dependence. The order does not legalize marijuana for medical or recreational use under federal law but significantly changes how it is regulated and researched.

The reclassification is a major shift for the cannabis industry, which has long operated under the burden of federal Schedule I status — the same classification as heroin and LSD. Supporters say the change will make it easier to study medicinal applications of marijuana and open new treatment options, while also providing financial relief to a struggling legal cannabis sector that has faced banking and tax disadvantages under federal law.

The decision drew immediate criticism from some Republicans. Sen. Tom Cotton (R-Ark.) posted on X that 'marijuana today is much more potent than just ten or twenty years ago, leading to increased psychosis, anti-social behavior, and fatal car crashes,' arguing the reclassification sends the wrong signal. Supporters, including cannabis industry advocates and some Democrats, welcomed the move as long overdue, noting that a majority of states have already legalized marijuana in some form.

The Trump administration's decision is notable given the party's historically tough-on-drugs stance, and reflects a broader, if gradual, federal shift away from heavy penalization of marijuana. The Biden administration had also initiated a review of marijuana's scheduling, but the formal reclassification order was ultimately signed under the current administration.

stories.headline: Millions of Americans May Qualify for Canadian Dual Citizenship Under New Law, Sparking Surge in Applications
stories.summary:
A change to Canadian citizenship law that took effect December 15 has opened dual citizenship eligibility to potentially millions of Americans with Canadian ancestors going back several generations. Previously, Canadian citizenship by descent could only be passed from parent to child; the new law extends eligibility to anyone who can prove a direct Canadian ancestor — a grandparent, great-grandparent, or more distant relative. Descendants are considered Canadian citizens under the law and need only submit proof to obtain a certificate of citizenship.

Immigration attorneys in both the United States and Canada say they have been overwhelmed with inquiries since the law took effect. Nicholas Berning, an immigration attorney at Boundary Bay Law in Bellingham, Washington, said his practice is 'pretty much flooded' with citizenship cases. A Vancouver-area attorney estimated his office went from roughly 200 citizenship cases per year to more than 20 consultations per day.

Americans cite a range of motivations for pursuing Canadian citizenship, including family heritage, job opportunities, and dissatisfaction with U.S. political conditions. Many applicants specifically reference President Donald Trump's immigration policies and other domestic political concerns as factors accelerating their decision to explore dual citizenship options.

The legal change reflects decades of evolving Canadian citizenship law aimed at addressing historic discrimination and updating outdated interpretations. Immigration experts say the full scale of eligible Americans has yet to be determined, but estimates suggest the number could reach into the millions given the extent of historical cross-border family ties.

stories.headline: Man Arrested in Florida with Handgun and Ammunition While Allegedly Heading to Shoot Up New Orleans Jazz Festival
stories.summary:
A man from Chapel Hill, North Carolina, was arrested Wednesday night at a hotel in Destin, Florida, after federal authorities identified him as planning a mass shooting at a large festival in Louisiana. The Okaloosa County Sheriff's Office said deputies recovered a handgun and approximately 200 rounds of ammunition from his hotel room. He was taken into custody without incident and will be extradited to Louisiana to face charges.

The arrest comes as the New Orleans Jazz & Heritage Festival — commonly known as JazzFest — is actively underway, running from Thursday through May 3. The annual event drew roughly 460,000 attendees last year. Louisiana State Police said the investigation continues in coordination with the FBI, adding that 'at this time, there are no known direct threats to any festivals in Louisiana.'

New Orleans Mayor Healena Moreno praised law enforcement, noting the coordination spanned multiple states from North Carolina to Florida. Federal authorities identified the alleged threat, though the specific agency was not named and the FBI's New Orleans office had not immediately responded to requests for comment.

The suspect is wanted on terroristic threats charges originating from Louisiana's Department of Public Safety. It was not immediately known whether he had retained legal counsel.

stories.headline: Trump Says King Charles's State Visit Could 'Absolutely' Help Repair U.S.-UK Relations, Criticizes Starmer on Iran and Immigration
stories.summary:
President Donald Trump told the BBC on Thursday that next week's state visit by King Charles III and Queen Camilla could help repair strained relations between the United States and the United Kingdom, calling the king 'a fantastic man' and 'a brave man.' The four-day visit begins Monday and will include a private meeting with Trump at the White House and an address by the king to Congress before the royal couple travels on to New York, Virginia, and Bermuda.

Trump also used the interview to sharply criticize British Prime Minister Keir Starmer, saying Starmer could only 'recover' politically if he changed course on immigration and opened up North Sea oil and gas extraction. Trump had previously called U.K. Ambassador Lord Mandelson 'a really bad pick,' and the two leaders have been at odds over Britain's refusal to join U.S. military operations against Iran.

Sir Keir pushed back Thursday, telling the BBC he makes decisions 'based on what's in the British national interest,' and defended his decision not to be 'dragged into the war in Iran.' Starmer said he would not be 'diverted or deflected' by outside pressure, including from Washington.

Trump claimed in the interview that the U.S. had effectively 'wiped Iran's military out' without needing allied support, describing his earlier calls for UK involvement as 'more of a test.' The remarks highlight ongoing tensions between the two governments even as the royal visit is framed by the Foreign Office as a celebration of the 250th anniversary of American independence and the countries' shared history.

stories.headline: Kalshi Suspends Three Political Candidates for Betting on Their Own Races
stories.summary:
Prediction market platform Kalshi announced Thursday that it had fined and suspended three political candidates after discovering they had placed bets on their own primary races. The platform, which operates legally in the United States, said the candidates violated its terms of service by trading on inside knowledge of their own electoral prospects.

The announcement raises fresh questions about the intersection of political campaigns and the fast-growing prediction market industry, which has expanded significantly in recent election cycles. Kalshi did not publicly identify the three candidates involved.

The incident highlights regulatory gaps around prediction markets and political participation. Critics have argued that allowing candidates to wager on their own outcomes creates obvious conflicts of interest, while supporters of prediction markets contend that existing platform rules and market mechanisms can self-police such behavior.

The story was reported by both CBS News and NBC News, drawing mainstream attention to what advocates and critics alike see as an emerging governance challenge as prediction markets become more prominent in U.S. political culture.

stories.headline: Iran's Exiled Crown Prince Reza Pahlavi Doused with Red Liquid at Berlin Press Conference
stories.summary:
Exiled Iranian Crown Prince Reza Pahlavi was struck with a red liquid Thursday as he departed a press conference in Berlin, Germany. Video footage of the incident circulated widely online, showing security personnel moving to restrain what appeared to be the protester responsible while others escorted Pahlavi away from the crowd.

Pahlavi, son of the late Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi who was deposed in the 1979 Islamic Revolution, has lived in exile and has been increasingly vocal in opposition to the current Iranian government, particularly as the country navigates a ceasefire following recent military conflict with the United States and Israel.

The attack comes at a sensitive moment for Iranian opposition figures abroad, as diplomatic negotiations over Iran's nuclear and military posture continue. Pahlavi has positioned himself as a potential leader for a post-Islamic Republic Iran, drawing both support from some Iranian diaspora communities and criticism from others who oppose monarchist restoration.

The identity and motive of the protester had not been confirmed in initial reports. The incident was covered by Bloomberg and The Hill.

stories.headline: MrBeast's Company Sued by Former Executive Over Sexual Harassment and Gender Bias Claims
stories.summary:
Beast Industries, the media company owned by YouTube star Jimmy Donaldson — known online as MrBeast — has been sued by a former senior employee who alleges widespread sexual harassment and gender discrimination at the firm. Lorrayne Mavromatis, who served as head of Instagram and later held a more senior role at the company from 2022 to 2025, claims in court documents that harassment of female employees was "condoned and/or perpetuated" by supervisors and that complaints were not taken seriously.

Among the specific allegations, Mavromatis claims a former Beast Industries CEO told her that her appearance had "a certain sexual effect on Jimmy" when she asked why Donaldson would not work with her on certain projects. She also alleges she was demoted after raising concerns, then fired less than three weeks after returning from pregnancy-related leave and told she was "too high-calibre" for her then-current role. She is seeking lost wages, lost benefits, reinstatement, and damages for emotional distress.

A representative for Beast Industries called the lawsuit a "clout-chasing complaint" built on "deliberate misrepresentations and categorically false statements," saying the company has messages, documents, and witness testimony to refute the claims. The company said the elimination of Mavromatis's role was part of a broader reorganization that affected both men and women.

Donaldson, who has more than 470 million YouTube subscribers and is the platform's most-followed creator, has previously faced legal challenges including allegations related to his reality show Beast Games. The lawsuit adds to scrutiny of the workplace culture inside one of the internet's largest media operations.

stories.headline: White House Accuses China of 'Industrial-Scale' Campaigns to Steal U.S. AI Secrets
stories.summary:
The Trump administration on Thursday formally accused China-backed actors of conducting "deliberate, industrial-scale campaigns" to distill and copy American frontier artificial intelligence models. White House Office of Science and Technology Policy Director Michael Kratsios issued a memo to federal agency heads outlining the accusation, escalating the rhetorical and policy confrontation between Washington and Beijing over AI dominance.

The memo marks one of the most direct government statements yet on Chinese efforts to acquire U.S. AI technology through illicit means, including through techniques such as model distillation, which involves training a smaller AI system using outputs from a more powerful one. The accusation comes as a former Google engineer was recently convicted of stealing AI trade secrets for China, a case cited by national security officials as emblematic of a broader threat.

The timing is notable: President Trump is expected to visit Beijing in the coming weeks, and the public accusation could complicate diplomatic preparations. The administration has not detailed specific remedies or sanctions tied to the memo, but the document signals that AI security will be a central friction point in U.S.-China relations going forward.

China has consistently denied accusations of state-sponsored intellectual property theft. The accusation adds to existing export controls on advanced semiconductors and AI chips that the U.S. has imposed on China, as both nations compete to lead in the development of next-generation AI systems.

stories.headline: JetBlue Hit With Class-Action Lawsuit Over Alleged 'Surveillance Pricing' Using Personal Data
stories.summary:
JetBlue Airways was sued in federal court in Brooklyn late Wednesday in a proposed class-action lawsuit alleging the carrier secretly uses customers' personal data — including browsing histories and location information — to set individualized ticket prices. The plaintiff, Andrew Phillips, claims JetBlue conceals the use of tracking software on its website and shares data with third parties whose programs help determine when to raise fares for specific users.

The lawsuit was triggered in part by an April 18 exchange on X in which a passenger complained about a $230 overnight price increase on a ticket needed for a funeral. JetBlue's official reply suggested the passenger try "clearing your cache and cookies or booking with an incognito window" — a response the company later walked back, saying it was incorrect. The episode drew immediate scrutiny from consumer advocates and lawmakers.

Two Democratic members of Congress sent letters to JetBlue this week demanding answers about its pricing practices, including whether it uses personal data to inform fares. The airline denied using personal data or artificial intelligence to set prices. JetBlue declined to comment on the lawsuit Thursday, and the company said fares fluctuate based on seat availability and demand.

The case cites alleged violations of a federal anti-wiretapping statute and New York state consumer protection laws, seeking unspecified damages. The lawsuit reflects growing regulatory and legal pressure on airlines and other industries over so-called surveillance pricing, a practice that has also drawn scrutiny at Delta Air Lines, which was questioned by lawmakers last year over its use of generative AI in pricing.

stories.headline: Glacier Serac Blocks Everest Route as Peak Climbing Season Begins, Threatening Dangerous Delays
stories.summary:
A massive, unstable block of glacier ice is obstructing the primary route up Mount Everest from Base Camp in Nepal, posing a serious threat to the 2026 spring climbing season. The obstruction, a roughly 100-foot-high serac located just below Camp 1, has halted the rope-fixing work that Sherpa "icefall doctors" must complete before climbers can safely ascend. By this point in April, ropes would normally already be fixed as far as Camp 3; teams are still blocked some 600 meters below Camp 1.

The Sagarmatha Pollution Control Committee, which coordinates rope-fixing operations, said it has found no safe way around or over the serac and that the only viable option is to wait for the ice to melt naturally, which officials hope could happen within days. Nepal's Department of Tourism said it is exploring whether to airlift rope-fixing teams and equipment directly to Camp 2 by helicopter, bypassing the obstruction and allowing preparation of the upper mountain to proceed in parallel.

The delay has heightened fears of dangerous overcrowding near the summit, a recurring problem on Everest in recent years. Favorable weather for summit attempts is typically limited to a narrow window before the end of May, meaning any prolonged blockage could compress the climbing season and push large numbers of expeditions into a short period simultaneously.

Nepal issues hundreds of climbing permits for Everest each spring, generating significant revenue for the country. Expedition teams already at Base Camp are waiting and acclimatizing, with prominent Nepali climber Purnima Shrestha, attempting her sixth Everest summit, warning from Base Camp that delays are compounding concerns about traffic jams on the upper mountain.

stories.headline: Warner Bros. Shareholders Approve $111 Billion Paramount Takeover
stories.summary:
Warner Bros. Discovery shareholders voted Thursday to approve a $111 billion takeover by Paramount, a deal that would reshape the American media landscape. The merged company would combine Warner Bros.' properties — including HBO Max, CNN, Harry Potter, and Game of Thrones — with Paramount's portfolio of CBS, Nickelodeon, Comedy Central, and Paramount+. Paramount is backed by tech billionaire Larry Ellison and led by his son David.

The deal still requires approval from the U.S. Department of Justice and European competition regulators before it can be finalized, with the companies targeting a September close. A dinner between Larry Ellison and President Trump is scheduled for Thursday evening at the U.S. Institute of Peace in Washington, drawing protests from activists who have raised concerns about the deal's regulatory pathway and the Ellisons' future stewardship of CNN. Trump has previously called CNN's leadership 'corrupt or incompetent' and said the network should be sold as part of any Warner Bros. transaction.

More than 1,400 entertainment industry figures — including Emma Thompson, Ben Stiller, and Javier Bardem — signed a letter in April warning the merger would produce fewer jobs, higher costs, and reduced creative opportunities. Paramount countered by pledging its commitment to talent and promising creators more avenues for their work, not fewer.

The deal's path through the Justice Department will be closely watched given the political dimensions of CNN's ownership. Netflix had previously submitted a rival bid for Warner Bros. before withdrawing after Paramount offered a higher price.

stories.headline: Senate Adopts GOP Budget Resolution, Clearing Path to Fund ICE and Reopen DHS
stories.summary:
The Senate adopted a Republican budget resolution Thursday following an overnight 'vote-a-rama' session lasting approximately six hours, advancing a plan that includes a $70 billion increase for immigration enforcement. The passage clears procedural groundwork for Republicans to fund Immigration and Customs Enforcement and address the ongoing Department of Homeland Security funding impasse without Democratic support.

Republicans beat back a series of Democratic amendments aimed at reducing costs and limiting enforcement spending during the marathon session. The vote-a-rama format, in which senators can offer unlimited amendments in rapid succession, allowed Democrats to force Republicans on record on a range of policy questions but ultimately failed to alter the resolution's core provisions.

Republicans framed the budget as a necessary step to restore border security and address what they called a breakdown in immigration enforcement under prior policy. Democrats argued the $70 billion enforcement increase prioritized deportations over programs affecting working families and accused Republicans of bypassing normal legislative negotiations.

The resolution sets the parameters for a forthcoming spending bill and represents a significant step in the prolonged standoff over DHS funding that has dominated Senate activity in recent days.

stories.headline: US-Kuwaiti Journalist Ahmed Shihab-Eldin Acquitted After 52 Days in Kuwaiti Detention
stories.summary:
American-Kuwaiti journalist Ahmed Shihab-Eldin was acquitted Thursday of all charges by a Kuwaiti court after being held for 52 days, according to his international legal team. Shihab-Eldin, who has contributed to the BBC, New York Times, and Al Jazeera, was arrested on March 3 after reposting on his Substack verified images and video of a U.S. F-15 fighter jet that crashed near Kuwait City after being mistakenly shot down by Kuwaiti air defenses.

He had faced charges of spreading false information, harming national security, and misusing his mobile phone. London-based barrister Caoilfhionn Gallagher KC confirmed the verdict Thursday, stating the legal team was relieved and focused on ensuring his liberty and safety. Kuwaiti authorities have not publicly commented on the case.

The case unfolded against the backdrop of the broader U.S.-Iran conflict, during which Kuwait and other Gulf Arab states detained hundreds of people for sharing footage documenting Iranian attacks on their territory. Kuwait's emir also issued a decree last month imposing prison sentences of up to 10 years for anyone who spreads information deemed to undermine confidence in military entities.

The Committee to Protect Journalists said it was 'relieved' by the acquittal but indicated it would continue monitoring the case. The arrest had drawn international attention as an example of press freedom being curtailed during wartime conditions in the Gulf region.

stories.headline: DOJ Reclassifies Cannabis as Schedule III Drug, Orders Broader Hearing
stories.summary:
The U.S. Department of Justice has officially reclassified certain cannabis products as Schedule III controlled substances, moving marijuana covered by FDA approval or state medical-marijuana licenses from Schedule I — the same category as heroin — to Schedule III, on par with Tylenol with codeine. Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche signed the order Thursday and also called for a hearing in June to consider reclassifying all marijuana more broadly.

The move fulfills a directive from President Trump, who instructed his administration last year to begin the reclassification process to expand access and enable more research. Blanche said the change would allow for scientific study of cannabis safety and efficacy, giving patients better care and doctors more reliable information. Marijuana nonetheless remains federally illegal, and the reclassification does not legalize its use or possession.

The rule change will be published in the Federal Register and takes effect after 30 days, during which it can be legally challenged — which is widely expected. Legal challenges could delay or block implementation for months or years. The Biden administration had initiated a similar review in 2022 and the DEA requested hearings in 2024, but those were indefinitely postponed.

Supporters of marijuana reform called the move largely symbolic but said it opens the door for more serious policy changes. Morgan Fox of the National Organization for the Reform of Marijuana Laws noted more than two-thirds of Americans support full legalization. The rescheduling comes five days after Trump signed a separate executive order boosting access to psychedelic drugs for medical treatment.

stories.headline: Two Trains Collide Head-On in Denmark, Five Critically Injured
stories.summary:
Two passenger trains collided head-on at a level crossing northwest of Copenhagen early Thursday morning, leaving five people critically injured and 13 others with less serious wounds. The crash occurred at approximately 6:29 a.m. local time on a line linking the towns of Hillerød and Kagerup in the North Zealand region, with 37 people on board the two trains combined.

Fire and rescue teams responded immediately, and critically injured passengers were airlifted to the National Hospital in Copenhagen. Public broadcaster DR showed images of the two yellow and grey trains facing each other in a wooded area, both with visible damage to the front. Several Lokaltog staff members were also among the injured.

Denmark's Accident Investigation Board arrived at the scene Thursday morning and is working with police to determine the cause. Investigators said they are examining all hypotheses, including a failure in the signalling system or human error. One railway expert suggested a driver may have overridden a stop signal while leaving a local station, and the Gribskov line is not believed to have an automated safety system.

Lokaltog safety director Claus Pedersson described the collision as 'one of the worst we can imagine in the railway industry.' Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen said she was 'very touched by the terrible train accident.' Rail accidents are considered rare in Denmark.

stories.headline: Update: Lebanese Journalist Killed in Israeli Airstrike After Being Trapped Under Rubble for Hours
stories.summary:
Lebanese journalist Amal Khalil, a reporter for the daily Al-Akhbar newspaper, was killed Wednesday in an Israeli airstrike on a house in the southern Lebanese village of al-Tiri where she had taken cover while reporting on the resumed Israel-Hezbollah conflict. Her body remained trapped under rubble for at least six hours after the strike because rescue workers were fired upon by Israeli forces when they attempted to reach her.

Khalil had been covering the conflict in Lebanon that resumed in early March. She and a colleague, Zeinab Faraj, had sought shelter in the house after an earlier Israeli airstrike struck near their vehicle. A second strike then hit the house directly. Faraj was seriously wounded and rescued earlier; the Lebanese Red Cross and civil defense were only able to retrieve Khalil's body shortly before midnight.

The Lebanese health ministry said the first strike killed two other people. Lebanon has accused Israel of committing war crimes in connection with the journalist's death. Israel's military has not publicly commented in detail on the circumstances of the strike.

Khalil's death adds to a rising toll of journalists killed covering conflicts in the region. Press freedom organizations have called for accountability. The killing comes amid the broader backdrop of ongoing U.S.-Israeli military operations against Iran and continued fighting along the Lebanon-Israel border.

stories.headline: Tanzania Officially Confirms 518 Deaths in Post-Election Violence
stories.summary:
Tanzania's government has released its first official death toll from violence following the October 2025 general election, confirming at least 518 people were killed. The announcement marks the first time authorities have publicly acknowledged the scale of the bloodshed that followed the disputed vote.

The post-election unrest drew widespread international condemnation and raised serious concerns about democratic backsliding in one of East Africa's largest nations. Opposition groups and human rights organizations had previously alleged that the true toll was being suppressed by the ruling party.

The release of an official figure, however delayed, represents a notable shift in the government's posture toward transparency on the crisis. Analysts say the acknowledgment could open the door to accountability processes, though critics warn that without independent verification, the figure may still undercount total casualties.

stories.headline: Update: DOJ Indicts Southern Poverty Law Center on Federal Fraud Charges Over Secret Paid Informant Program
stories.summary:
Federal prosecutors have announced fraud charges against the Southern Poverty Law Center, the prominent civil rights organization known for tracking hate groups. Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche said the SPLC secretly funneled more than $3 million to individuals associated with violent extremist organizations, including the KKK, the neo-Nazi National Alliance, and the National Socialist Movement, between 2014 and 2023. The indictment includes six counts of wire fraud, four counts of bank fraud, and one count of conspiracy to commit money laundering.

Among the most striking allegations: the SPLC sent more than $270,000 to a person who helped plan and attended the deadly 2017 Unite the Right rally in Charlottesville, Virginia. In another case, more than $1 million was paid over nine years to someone who had infiltrated and stolen documents from the National Alliance. The DOJ alleges the organization misrepresented to donors how their contributions would be used.

SPLC interim leader Bryan Fair said the organization would 'vigorously defend ourselves, our staff, and our work,' calling the prosecution the latest example of the Trump administration targeting civil rights groups. He noted that the use of informants 'was necessary because we are no stranger to threats of violence,' citing a 1983 firebombing of the group's offices.

The case is politically charged. Conservatives have long accused the SPLC of unfairly designating mainstream right-leaning organizations as hate groups, while supporters of the SPLC argue the charges represent a politically motivated assault on one of the country's most storied civil rights institutions. The FBI severed its relationship with the SPLC in October, calling it a 'partisan smear machine.'

stories.headline: Insider Bets on Orban's Fall Traced to Fund Linked to Hungarian PM's Family
stories.summary:
An asset-management fund within the Equilor group of companies, which is partly owned by Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban's son-in-law, made substantial financial-market bets that Orban would lose power. The revelation raises significant questions about whether individuals at the heart of Orban's own political and financial network had advance knowledge of, or diminishing confidence in, his political future.

Orban has governed Hungary since 2010 and is widely regarded as one of Europe's most entrenched authoritarian leaders, having consolidated control over the judiciary, media, and electoral system. The discovery that a fund linked to his inner circle was wagering against his continued rule is a potentially damaging political embarrassment.

The report adds to growing scrutiny of Orban's family financial interests and the overlapping networks of business and political power in Hungary. Opposition figures and EU officials have long argued that Orban's governance has blurred the lines between state resources and personal enrichment for those close to him.

stories.headline: Senate Votes in Early Morning Session, Making Incremental Progress on DHS Shutdown
stories.summary:
The U.S. Senate held a late-night vote-a-rama session, producing a small but notable sign of progress toward resolving the two-month Department of Homeland Security shutdown. A 3 a.m. vote indicated that some bipartisan movement may be possible after weeks of deadlock over DHS funding.

The shutdown, which has left significant portions of DHS operations in limbo, has been a source of growing concern among lawmakers on both sides of the aisle, with border security and immigration enforcement operations among those affected. Pressure has mounted on congressional leadership to reach a resolution.

Details of what specifically advanced in the overnight session were limited, but the development suggests negotiations are continuing at the staff and leadership level. The outcome of any eventual funding agreement could have broad implications for immigration policy and federal law enforcement operations heading into the summer.

stories.headline: Johnson Faces Pressure From Both Parties as DHS Funding Deadline Looms
stories.summary:
Speaker Mike Johnson is under intensifying pressure from Republicans and Democrats alike as the Department of Homeland Security approaches a deadline at which it would lack funds to pay employees. Johnson is insisting on passing a GOP-only bill to fund immigration enforcement before taking up broader, bipartisan legislation to fund the rest of DHS — a sequencing that has put him at odds with Senate Republicans who want a more sweeping election-year affordability package.

Senate Republicans and House Republicans are on a collision course over whether to use a budget vehicle that Democrats cannot block to narrowly fund DHS immigration operations or to pursue a larger legislative push. Senate Republicans posted on social media that 'Democrats voted to defund law enforcement 16+ times in the last 68 days,' while Democrats have said they will not approve a DHS budget without accompanying reforms.

The standoff reflects broader tensions within the Republican conference over immigration strategy heading into the 2026 midterm elections. Democrats argue that Republicans are manufacturing a funding crisis to score political points on border security, while Republicans contend that Democrats are blocking essential funding for immigration enforcement officers.

stories.headline: South African Court Orders Zambia to Return Former President Lungu's Body Hours After Transfer
stories.summary:
A South African court has ordered Zambia's government to return the remains of former President Edgar Lungu just hours after Zambia announced it had taken formal possession of the body following a court transfer. The reversal adds a new legal twist to a months-long dispute over the remains of Lungu, who died aged 68 at a clinic in Pretoria from an undisclosed illness.

Zambia's government had stated late Wednesday that Lungu's remains were 'formally transferred' to the state after his family was reportedly unable to proceed with an appeals case. But a South African court subsequently ordered the body returned, and the funeral home where the remains had been kept said it would no longer accept them, leaving the situation in legal limbo.

The dispute is rooted in a long-standing feud between Lungu, who led Zambia from 2015 to 2021, and his successor President Hakainde Hichilema. Lungu's family had sought a private burial and said the former president did not want Hichilema present at any funeral proceedings. Zambia's government maintained that, as a former head of state, Lungu was entitled to a state burial alongside predecessors in Lusaka.

stories.headline: Sri Lanka Investigates $2.5 Million Hack of Finance Ministry Computer Systems
stories.summary:
Sri Lanka is investigating the theft of $2.5 million from government accounts after hackers breached the Finance Ministry's computer systems, Treasury Secretary Harshana Suriyapperuma confirmed Thursday. The breach represents a significant cybersecurity failure for a country that has only recently emerged from a severe economic crisis that required an IMF bailout.

Few additional details about the attackers or the methods used have been publicly disclosed, and the investigation is ongoing. The incident raises questions about the security of government financial infrastructure in developing economies that are undergoing rapid digital transformation.

Sri Lanka's vulnerability to such attacks comes as the country continues efforts to rebuild fiscal credibility with international creditors and restructure its sovereign debt following its 2022 default. A breach of Finance Ministry systems could undermine confidence in the country's financial governance at a critical juncture.

stories.headline: UK Chancellor Reeves Posts Lowest Deficit Since Labour Took Power on Surge in Wealth Tax Revenue
stories.summary:
British Chancellor Rachel Reeves has recorded the United Kingdom's lowest budget deficit since Labour came to power in 2024, driven in large part by revenue from higher wealth taxes that the government introduced after taking office. The tax increases, which targeted Britain's more affluent population, generated more than £30 billion, according to new figures.

Reeves and the Labour government had pledged a clampdown on wealth and capital at the upper end of the income scale as a central plank of their fiscal strategy. Critics, including business groups and Conservative opponents, had warned that higher wealth taxes could prompt capital flight or dampen investment, arguments the new revenue figures will complicate.

The deficit figures provide political ammunition for the government ahead of ongoing debates about the UK's fiscal trajectory. However, economists caution that a single year of stronger-than-expected tax receipts does not necessarily resolve the longer-term structural pressures on public finances, including rising debt service costs and health and pension spending.

stories.headline: Human Remains of Three Young Children Discovered in Memphis Woods
stories.summary:
The remains of three children, estimated to be between the ages of 3 and 7, have been found in a heavily wooded area in the Hickory Hill neighborhood of southwest Memphis, Tennessee. The investigation began March 8 after an anonymous caller reported finding a human skull near the woods. Cadaver dogs and a subsequent drainage system search led to the discovery of additional skulls and 14 bones consistent with human remains.

Memphis Police Chief Cerelyn Davis announced the findings at a press conference Wednesday, confirming that initial medical examiner analysis identified the remains as belonging to three children. Approximately 170 personnel, including FBI agents, are involved in the ongoing search of the crime scene. Investigators have not yet determined the identities of the children or the cause of death.

Authorities are urging anyone with information to contact police, noting that the case is deeply concerning to the community. Chief Davis stressed there is no indication of an active public threat but called on the public for help, saying: 'Somebody knows where these children have come from. Someone knows the missing children that we have discovered in this area.'

stories.headline: Medical Examiner Rules Death of Teen Found in Singer D4vd's Car a Homicide
stories.summary:
The Los Angeles County medical examiner's office has ruled the death of 14-year-old Celeste Rivas Hernandez a homicide, announcing Wednesday that she died of multiple penetrating injuries caused by an undisclosed object or objects. Her body was found in September in the front trunk of a Tesla registered to TikTok star and singer D4vd, whose real name is David Anthony Burke, 21. Stab wounds were found on her abdomen and chest during the autopsy, and shredded blue plastic was discovered in some of her wounds.

Prosecutors allege Rivas Hernandez was killed and her body dismembered on or around May 5, 2025, roughly four months before she was found. A toxicology report indicated the presence of methamphetamine or MDMA and low levels of alcohol, but noted neither appeared to play a role in her death. Burke has been charged with murder and pleaded not guilty; his attorneys say they will 'vigorously defend his innocence' and contend the evidence will show he did not kill her.

The medical examiner's office had determined the cause of death in December but was barred from releasing findings publicly during the police investigation. Chief Medical Examiner Dr. Odey Ukpo expressed frustration at the delay, calling it 'unfathomable' that the family had to wait so long. Burke is scheduled to appear in Los Angeles court Thursday morning.

stories.headline: Update: RFK Jr. Concludes Marathon Congressional Hearings, Deflects Blame for Measles Outbreaks
stories.summary:
Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. wrapped up a multiday series of congressional budget hearings Wednesday, during which he defended the Trump administration's proposed 2027 budget that would cut more than 12% of Department of Health and Human Services funding. Kennedy acknowledged the cuts were 'painful' but argued they were necessary given the federal government's $39 trillion deficit. Lawmakers from both parties raised concerns about reductions to research funding and public health programs.

A central point of contention was responsibility for declining childhood vaccination rates and ongoing measles outbreaks across the country. Kennedy repeatedly denied culpability, saying 'It has nothing to do with me,' and pointed to rising measles cases globally in countries including Canada, Mexico, and the United Kingdom. He disputed characterizations of him as anti-vaccine, saying he is 'pro-science,' though he spent years publicly questioning vaccine safety and in 2021 urged people to 'resist' CDC guidelines.

Democratic lawmakers accused Kennedy of spreading misinformation and causing a 'spillover effect' on public health trust, while Kennedy accused Democrats of grandstanding and seeking sound bites. Republicans largely backed his framing of HHS restructuring as fiscally necessary. Surveys cited during the hearings show trust in federal health agencies has continued to decline since Kennedy took office, a trend he attributed to erosion that began during the COVID-19 pandemic.

stories.headline: Nigeria's Suspected Coup Plotters Plead Not Guilty to Treason Charges
stories.summary:
Six people, including a retired major general and a serving police inspector, appeared in Nigeria's Federal High Court in Abuja on Wednesday and pleaded not guilty to charges of treason, terrorism, and money laundering for allegedly plotting to overthrow President Bola Tinubu. The defendants were ordered to remain in the custody of Nigeria's domestic intelligence agency, the Department of State Services. A seventh alleged conspirator, former petroleum minister and ex-governor Timipre Sylva, remains at large.

Rumors of the plot first surfaced in October 2025 when Nigeria abruptly canceled a military parade marking the country's 65th Independence Anniversary, with officials citing security concerns. In January, the military announced 16 officers would face a separate military tribunal. The civilians and retired military personnel now facing civilian court charges are accused of conspiring with a serving army colonel to 'levy war against the state to overawe' the president.

Defense lawyers complained during Wednesday's proceedings that they had been denied access to their clients since September 2025, suggesting the defendants may have been in custody for months before charges were formally filed. Prosecutors allege money changed hands linked to terrorism financing, with bail hearings scheduled for April 27. Nigeria has experienced multiple coups since independence, making the case a significant test of the country's democratic institutions.

stories.headline: Update: Billionaire Justin Sun Sues Trump Family's World Liberty Crypto Firm Over Alleged Token Seizure
stories.summary:
Billionaire crypto entrepreneur Justin Sun has filed a lawsuit in San Francisco federal court accusing World Liberty Financial, the crypto venture co-founded by President Donald Trump and his son Eric Trump, of freezing his digital tokens and threatening to destroy them without justification. Sun, who initially invested $45 million in the firm, alleges the company has blocked him from selling or voting his WLFI tokens — which at their peak were valued at more than $1 billion — in what he calls an 'illegal scheme.' The price of a single WLFI token has fallen from 31 cents to under 8 cents since September.

Sun alleges that co-founder Chase Herro and others are using the Trump brand to 'profit through fraud,' and that early promises allowing token holders to eventually trade their holdings were 'false and misleading.' He also purchased $100 million of Trump's meme coins in July 2025. World Liberty denied wrongdoing, with co-founder Zach Witkoff — son of Trump Middle East envoy Steve Witkoff — calling the suit a 'desperate attempt to deflect attention from Sun's own misconduct.' Eric Trump dismissed the lawsuit as 'ridiculous.'

The case adds to scrutiny surrounding the Trump family's cryptocurrency ventures and their intersection with foreign investors. The Securities and Exchange Commission separately dropped its own investigation into Sun earlier this year. The new development in this previously reported story is the formal filing of Sun's federal complaint, which adds specific legal allegations including details about the token freezing, vote-stripping, and threatened destruction of his holdings.
